title: Applying QED to Hardware Accelerator Verification. Innovation in Verification - Semiwiki
gist: A-QED2 with functional decomposition makes formal verification of large AI/arithmetic hardware accelerators (including NVIDIA's NVDLA) tractable, cutting verification effort ~5x on average and up to 9x on the largest designs while catching bugs conventional bounded model checking misses.

themes.theme: Functional consistency as a bug-finding method

insights.claim: Functional consistency checking verifies hardware by comparing a design against a second symbolic execution of itself, asking whether the same input can ever produce two different outputs, without needing a functional spec.
insights.speaker: Bernard Murphy
insights.so_what: A spec-free verification method scales to accelerators where writing golden reference models is impractical, lowering verification cost for AI silicon.
insights.quote: sort of equivalence checking against yourself, the design is compared with a second execution of itself

insights.claim: Functional consistency alone cannot prove correctness because a design that always produces the wrong answer is still consistent, so A-QED pairs it with Single-Action Correctness checks that require a reference spec for at least one execution per action.
insights.speaker: Bernard Murphy
insights.so_what: Buyers of formal tools should note FC needs a correctness complement; the combined FC+SAC criterion is what makes the method sound.
insights.quote: A design that always produces the wrong answer is still functionally consistent.
insights.locator: Of course, FC alone cannot prove correctness

insights.claim: The core idea of exploiting self-consistency for verification dates to 1996 and matured through SQED (Symbolic Quick Error Detection) in processor verification before A-QED adapted it to modern loosely coupled accelerators.
insights.speaker: Bernard Murphy
insights.so_what: The technique is decades-mature, reducing adoption risk for EDA vendors like Cadence productizing it.
insights.locator: The core idea of exploiting self-consistency

insights.claim: A-QED2 makes formal proofs scale by decomposing an accelerator into sub-functions so the consistency proof is pushed down into each block, and the decomposition strategy differs for consistency (FC), correctness (SAC), and latency (Response Bound) checks.
insights.speaker: Bernard Murphy
insights.so_what: Decomposition is the key that turns intractable formal runs into seconds-to-minutes, the commercial hook for verifying billion-gate AI accelerators.
insights.locator: This paper, Scaling Up Hardware Accelerator Verification

insights.claim: If an accelerator's decomposition is proved both functionally consistent and conformant to spec for a single input, it is necessarily functionally correct over all sequences of multiple inputs.
insights.speaker: Bernard Murphy
insights.so_what: Single-input proofs generalizing to all input sequences is what makes the approach practically fast, a differentiator versus exhaustive bounded model checking.
insights.locator: The other key contribution of the paper

insights.claim: The SAC decomposition step still forces engineers to manually derive preconditions, invariants, and postconditions, which the reviewer calls the least satisfying part of the approach.
insights.speaker: Bernard Murphy
insights.so_what: Manual correctness decomposition remains a labor bottleneck, a gap a future EDA product or automation could monetize.
insights.quote: perhaps the least satisfying aspect of the approach

insights.claim: On 17 loosely coupled accelerator testcases in the 300-600k gate range, functional consistency without decomposition completed only 2 within 12 hours on a 128GB Xeon server, while with decomposition 10 completed in under a few minutes.
insights.speaker: Bernard Murphy
insights.so_what: Two-orders-of-magnitude runtime collapse is the quantified value proposition for adopting decomposed formal verification.
insights.quote: Without decomposition, only 2 testcases complete within 12 hours on a Xeon server with 128GB RAM, with decomposition 10 complete in under a few minutes.
insights.locator: The authors apply A-QED2 to 17 LCA testcases

insights.claim: Across 10 completed testcases containing 90 injected bugs, spec-blind functional consistency found 49 of them while running in seconds.
insights.speaker: Bernard Murphy
insights.so_what: Catching ~54% of bugs with zero spec knowledge in seconds shows FC as a cheap high-yield first-pass screen.
insights.quote: Across the 10 testcases that complete there are 90 different bugs to catch. FC finds 49
insights.locator: The authors run on multiple buggy versions

insights.claim: A-QED2 was demonstrated on 109 buggy versions of 16 accelerators ranging from thousands to over 200 million gates including NVIDIA's NVDLA, detecting all bugs found by conventional simulation and reducing verification effort about 5x on average and 9x on the largest industrial designs.
insights.speaker: Bernard Murphy
insights.so_what: Proven at 200M+ gate industrial scale with NVIDIA IP validates the method for real AI-accelerator verification workloads.
insights.quote: reduced overall verification effort by about 5x on average, reaching 9x for the largest industrial designs

insights.claim: Despite strong results, A-QED has only 16 citations and the reviewer notes it has received surprisingly little attention in the literature.
insights.speaker: Bernard Murphy
insights.so_what: Underappreciated technique signals a possible early-mover advantage for EDA vendors productizing it before it becomes standard.
insights.quote: A-QED [16 citations] has received surprisingly little attention in the literature
insights.locator: Despite the elegance of the approach

insights.claim: Response Bound verification checks that an accelerator produces output within a predefined latency bound, with failures indicating deadlock, livelock, or scheduling bugs that correctness checks alone would miss.
insights.speaker: Bernard Murphy
insights.so_what: Timing/responsiveness verification is a distinct axis buyers need beyond functional correctness for real-time AI inference hardware.
insights.quote: Failure indicates deadlock, livelock, scheduling errors, or other responsiveness bugs.
